items.prompt_md: In the O(1)-min stack design, what does `min_stack[-1]` represent at any point in time?
options: The smallest value ever pushed, even if it has since been popped
The minimum of only the values currently on the data stack
The average of the top two elements
The index of the smallest element in the data stack
items.answer: The minimum of only the values currently on the data stack
items.explanation_md: Because `min_stack` is popped in lockstep with the data stack, its top always reflects the minimum of what's still there — not of everything that was ever pushed.

items.prompt_md: When pushing a new value `val`, what value gets appended to `min_stack`?
options: `val` itself, unconditionally
The minimum of `val` and the previous top of `min_stack` (or just `val` if `min_stack` was empty)
The previous top of `min_stack`, unchanged
The maximum of `val` and the previous top of `min_stack`
items.answer: The minimum of `val` and the previous top of `min_stack` (or just `val` if `min_stack` was empty)
items.explanation_md: Each slot in `min_stack` is a snapshot of 'the minimum including this push,' so it must compare the incoming value against whatever the running minimum was just before it.

items.prompt_md: Why must `pop()` remove from both `stack` and `min_stack` together, rather than only from `stack`?
options: Because leaving a stale value on top of `min_stack` would report the wrong minimum for what remains
Because `min_stack` is only for debugging and doesn't affect correctness
Because popping only `stack` would raise an error
It doesn't matter — either way produces the same result
items.answer: Because leaving a stale value on top of `min_stack` would report the wrong minimum for what remains
items.explanation_md: If `min_stack` weren't popped too, its top could still reflect a value that includes an element that's no longer on the data stack, breaking the 'minimum of what's currently there' guarantee.

items.prompt_md: A buggy min-stack only appends to `min_stack` when the new value is smaller than the current minimum, skipping the append otherwise. What breaks?
options: `stack` and `min_stack` fall out of sync in length, so popping eventually pops the wrong minimum snapshot
Nothing breaks — this is just a memory optimization
`get_min` becomes O(n) instead of O(1)
`push` becomes O(log n) instead of O(1)
items.answer: `stack` and `min_stack` fall out of sync in length, so popping eventually pops the wrong minimum snapshot
items.explanation_md: The whole trick depends on both stacks having exactly one entry per push, in lockstep — skipping an append on `min_stack` misaligns it with `stack` and corrupts every `get_min` after that point.

items.prompt_md: Why can't `get_min` just be implemented as `min(self.stack)` and still meet the design's requirement?
options: Because `min(self.stack)` scans every element, which is O(n), not the required O(1)
Because Python's `min()` doesn't work on lists of integers
Because it would return the wrong value
There's no difference in complexity between the two
items.answer: Because `min(self.stack)` scans every element, which is O(n), not the required O(1)
items.explanation_md: `min(self.stack)` is correct but re-derives the answer from scratch every call; the whole point of the shadow stack is to avoid that scan by carrying the answer forward instead.

items.prompt_md: In the two-stacks-as-a-queue design, what are the two stacks typically called and what does each hold?
options: An 'in' stack that receives newly enqueued values, and an 'out' stack that serves dequeues in FIFO order
A 'sorted' stack and an 'unsorted' stack
A 'read' stack and a 'write' stack that always mirror each other exactly
A 'min' stack and a 'max' stack
items.answer: An 'in' stack that receives newly enqueued values, and an 'out' stack that serves dequeues in FIFO order
items.explanation_md: Enqueues always push onto `in`; dequeues pop from `out`, refilling `out` from `in` (which reverses the order back to FIFO) whenever `out` runs dry.

items.prompt_md: In the two-stack queue, when does the `in` stack get transferred onto the `out` stack?
options: Only when `out` is empty and a dequeue (or peek) is requested
After every single enqueue, unconditionally
Only when `in` becomes full
Never — `in` and `out` are never connected
items.answer: Only when `out` is empty and a dequeue (or peek) is requested
items.explanation_md: Transferring lazily — only when `out` has nothing left to serve — is exactly what keeps the amortized cost low; transferring on every enqueue would make every operation O(n).

items.prompt_md: Why does popping everything from `in` and pushing it onto `out` restore FIFO order?
options: Because popping a stack reverses order, and reversing a reversed order (the enqueue order) gives back the original order
It doesn't restore FIFO order — the two-stack queue is actually LIFO
Because `out` is sorted automatically after the transfer
Because only the top element of `in` is moved, not all of it
items.answer: Because popping a stack reverses order, and reversing a reversed order (the enqueue order) gives back the original order
items.explanation_md: `in` holds enqueue order top-to-bottom reversed (newest on top); popping it one by one and pushing onto `out` flips that reversal, so `out`'s top is now the oldest enqueued value — the correct next dequeue.

items.prompt_md: A buggy two-stack queue transfers from `in` to `out` on every enqueue call, instead of only when `out` is empty. What's the consequence?
options: Every enqueue becomes O(n) instead of O(1), since it re-does a full transfer each time
The queue starts behaving like a stack instead of a queue
Dequeue would raise an error immediately
There is no consequence — the results are identical either way
items.answer: Every enqueue becomes O(n) instead of O(1), since it re-does a full transfer each time
items.explanation_md: The results would still be correct, but the performance guarantee is gone — transferring unconditionally on every enqueue turns a cheap O(1) push into an O(n) rescan every time.

items.prompt_md: What must `dequeue` and `peek` check before reading from `out`?
options: Whether `out` is empty, and if so, transfer everything from `in` into `out` first
Whether `in` is empty, and if so, refuse the operation
Whether the queue has more than one element
Nothing — `out` can always be read directly
items.answer: Whether `out` is empty, and if so, transfer everything from `in` into `out` first
items.explanation_md: Reading from an empty `out` without first pulling from `in` would either error or wrongly report the queue as empty, even though `in` might be holding elements.

items.prompt_md: What invariant do both the min-stack and the two-stack-queue designs share, even though they solve different problems?
options: Neither design ever pushes more than one element at a time
An auxiliary stack is kept in sync with (or derived from) the primary stack so an expensive query becomes a cheap O(1) read
Both designs sort their contents before answering any query
Both designs require a hash map as the auxiliary structure
items.answer: An auxiliary stack is kept in sync with (or derived from) the primary stack so an expensive query becomes a cheap O(1) read
items.explanation_md: In both cases, the trick is the same: pay a little extra bookkeeping on push/pop so that a query that would otherwise need a rescan becomes a single peek — a shadow stack for the min, an `out` stack for FIFO order.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of `push`, `pop`, and `get_min` in the augmented min-stack design?
options: O(1) for all three
O(n) for get_min only, O(1) for push and pop
O(log n) for all three
O(n) for push and pop, O(1) for get_min
items.answer: O(1) for all three
items.explanation_md: Each operation only touches the top of one or both stacks — no scanning is ever needed, so push, pop, top, and get_min are all O(1), at the cost of O(n) extra space for the shadow stack.

items.prompt_md: What is the worst-case time complexity of a single `dequeue` call in the two-stack queue, and when does it occur?
options: O(n), on the call that triggers a full transfer from `in` to `out` because `out` was empty
O(1) always, with no exceptions
O(log n), because the transfer is done via binary search
O(n^2), because every transferred element is compared against every other
items.answer: O(n), on the call that triggers a full transfer from `in` to `out` because `out` was empty
items.explanation_md: A dequeue that finds `out` empty must move every element currently in `in`, which is O(n) in the worst case — but that expensive call is rare relative to how many cheap calls precede it.

items.prompt_md: Even though a single dequeue can cost O(n) when it triggers a transfer, what is the *amortized* time complexity of dequeue across a long sequence of operations, and why?
options: O(1) amortized, because each element is only ever moved from `in` to `out` once over its entire lifetime in the queue
O(n) amortized, since transfers happen constantly
O(log n) amortized, since transfers are balanced like a tree
O(n^2) amortized, since every dequeue re-transfers everything
items.answer: O(1) amortized, because each element is only ever moved from `in` to `out` once over its entire lifetime in the queue
items.explanation_md: Every element is pushed onto `in` once, moved to `out` at most once, and popped from `out` once — three constant-cost touches per element total, so spread over n operations the average cost per call is O(1).

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the two-stack queue design relative to the number of elements currently enqueued, n?
options: O(n), since every enqueued element is stored in exactly one of the two stacks at any time
O(1), since only pointers are stored
O(n^2), since elements are duplicated across both stacks
O(log n), since the stacks are compressed
items.answer: O(n), since every enqueued element is stored in exactly one of the two stacks at any time
items.explanation_md: Each element lives in `in` or `out`, never both at once, so the total space used scales linearly with however many elements are currently in the queue.

items.prompt_md: You need a stack supporting push, pop, and a `get_min()` call that must run in O(1) — not by rescanning the stack's contents. Which design fits?
options: Keep the stack's contents sorted at all times
Maintain a second, parallel stack that stores the running minimum alongside each pushed value
Recompute the minimum with a full scan every time `get_min` is called
Track values in a hash map keyed by value, and scan its keys for the smallest
items.answer: Maintain a second, parallel stack that stores the running minimum alongside each pushed value
items.explanation_md: A shadow stack that mirrors every push with "the minimum including this value" turns `get_min` into a single O(1) peek, and `pop` still works in O(1) because popping both stacks together restores the correct earlier minimum for free.

items.prompt_md: You're asked to implement a first-in-first-out queue, but you're only given access to a stack data structure (push/pop from one end) to build it with.
options: Use two stacks: push onto an 'in' stack, and transfer everything to an 'out' stack (reversing order back to FIFO) whenever a dequeue finds 'out' empty
Sort the stack's contents before every dequeue
Use a hash map from insertion order to value
Use a single stack and just pop from the bottom instead of the top
items.answer: Use two stacks: push onto an 'in' stack, and transfer everything to an 'out' stack (reversing order back to FIFO) whenever a dequeue finds 'out' empty
items.explanation_md: Two stacks used this way flip the order twice — once going into 'in', once transferring to 'out' — which cancels out and restores FIFO order, with each element moved at most once for O(1) amortized cost.

items.prompt_md: You need a stack that supports push, pop, and a `get_max()` query, all in O(1), where `get_max` must reflect only the values currently on the stack.
options: Maintain a shadow stack tracking the running maximum, popped in lockstep with the main stack
Recompute the maximum by scanning the stack on every `get_max` call
Sort the stack's contents after every push
Use a hash map counting how many times each value occurs
items.answer: Maintain a shadow stack tracking the running maximum, popped in lockstep with the main stack
items.explanation_md: This is the min-stack trick mirrored for maximum — the aggregate you need only depends on what's still on the stack, so a synchronized shadow stack gives O(1) without ever rescanning.

items.prompt_md: You're designing a text editor's undo/redo feature, where each edit is pushed as it happens, and 'undo' must always revert the single most recent edit still in effect.
options: Use a stack of edits, popping the most recent one on undo (optionally pushing it onto a second stack for redo)
Use a hash map from edit id to edit content
Sort edits by timestamp and binary search for the most recent
Use two pointers scanning the edit history from both ends
items.answer: Use a stack of edits, popping the most recent one on undo (optionally pushing it onto a second stack for redo)
items.explanation_md: 'Undo the most recently applied thing' is a LIFO relationship by definition, and pairing it with a second stack for redo is the same auxiliary-stack pattern used for min-stack and O(1) aggregates.

items.prompt_md: You need a data structure supporting push, pop, and a `get_median()` query that must reflect only the currently-held values, ideally faster than a full rescan each time.
options: Maintain two heaps (or a balanced structure) tracking the lower and upper halves, since a single auxiliary stack can't reorder values on removal from the middle
Maintain a shadow stack storing the running median at each push, popped in lockstep
Use a hash map counting frequencies of each value
Sort the stack after every operation
items.answer: Maintain two heaps (or a balanced structure) tracking the lower and upper halves, since a single auxiliary stack can't reorder values on removal from the middle
items.explanation_md: Unlike min or max, the median can change based on values in the *middle* of the collection, not just the top — a stack's lockstep-pop trick only works for aggregates that shrink monotonically as the top is removed, so this needs a different structure entirely, not the augmented-stack pattern.

items.prompt_md: You need to design a structure that supports insert, remove-by-value, and get-random-element, all in O(1) average time.
options: Use a hash map from value to its index in a dynamic array, swapping-and-popping the array on removal to keep it dense
Maintain a shadow stack tracking a running random seed
Push and pop values on a stack, returning the top for get-random
Use two stacks, one for insertion order and one for removal order
items.answer: Use a hash map from value to its index in a dynamic array, swapping-and-popping the array on removal to keep it dense
items.explanation_md: 'Random access plus O(1) removal by value' needs direct indexing and lookup by value, which is a hash-map-plus-array design — there's no LIFO relationship here for a stack (shadow or otherwise) to exploit.

items.prompt_md: You're designing a cache that must support get and put in O(1), evicting the least-recently-used entry when it's full.
options: Combine a hash map (for O(1) lookup) with a doubly linked list (for O(1) reordering of recency)
Maintain an auxiliary stack tracking least-recently-used order
Push every access onto a stack and evict from the bottom
Sort entries by last-access time before every eviction
items.answer: Combine a hash map (for O(1) lookup) with a doubly linked list (for O(1) reordering of recency)
items.explanation_md: LRU needs O(1) *arbitrary* reordering (any entry can jump to 'most recent' on access, not just the top), which a stack's top-only access can't provide — the hash-map-plus-linked-list combo is the standard tool here.

items.prompt_md: You're given a stream of values and need to answer, at any moment, how many distinct values have been seen so far.
options: Keep a hash set of values seen so far and report its size
Maintain a shadow stack tracking the count alongside each push
Use two stacks to track seen and unseen values separately
Push every value and pop duplicates as they arrive
items.answer: Keep a hash set of values seen so far and report its size
items.explanation_md: 'How many distinct values so far' only needs membership tracking, not an ordering or LIFO relationship, so a hash set answers it directly — no stack design is needed.

items.prompt_md: You need to evaluate a fully parenthesized arithmetic expression given in postfix (Reverse Polish) notation, where each operator applies to the values immediately preceding it.
options: Push operands onto a stack, and on each operator pop the top two, apply the operator, and push the result back
Use a hash map from operator to a precomputed result
Sort the tokens before evaluating
Use two pointers scanning the token list from both ends
items.answer: Push operands onto a stack, and on each operator pop the top two, apply the operator, and push the result back
items.explanation_md: Postfix evaluation is a design classic: the two values an operator needs are always whatever's most recently pushed, so a stack holding pending operands (and pushing back each computed result) handles it directly.

items.prompt_md: You need a browser's back/forward navigation history, where visiting a new page clears the forward history, and 'back' must always return to the single most recently visited page.
options: Use two stacks — one for back history, one for forward history — moving pages between them as the user navigates
Use a hash map from URL to visit count
Sort visited pages by timestamp and binary search
Use a sliding window over the last k visited pages
items.answer: Use two stacks — one for back history, one for forward history — moving pages between them as the user navigates
items.explanation_md: 'Undo to the most recent, with a symmetric redo' is the same paired-stack shape as undo/redo and the two-stack queue: each stack tracks 'what's LIFO-available in that direction.'

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether two given sequences of stack push/pop operations are consistent with each other — i.e., whether the claimed pop order could actually result from the claimed push order on one real stack.
options: Simulate a single stack: push in the given order, greedily popping whenever the next expected popped value sits on top
Use a hash map counting how many times each value is pushed versus popped
Sort both sequences and compare them position by position
Use two independent stacks, one per sequence, and compare their final contents
items.answer: Simulate a single stack: push in the given order, greedily popping whenever the next expected popped value sits on top
items.explanation_md: This is a direct behavioral question about a single stack's mechanics, so simulating one real stack and checking whether it can greedily reproduce the given pop order is the exact tool for the job.
